words.shortDefinition: Scalable virtual servers for running applications in the AWS cloud environment.
words.simpleDefinition: Resizable virtual compute capacity — launch and configure servers without physical hardware management. Developers control OS, networking, and storage, paying only for the instance time and resources consumed by their workloads.
words.longDefinition: Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) provides a broad selection of instance types optimized to fit different use cases. These include varied combinations of CPU, memory, storage, and networking capacity, giving you the flexibility to choose the appropriate mix of resources for your applications. Instances can be launched across different regions and availability zones to ensure high availability and fault tolerance for production environments.
words.connector: Think of EC2 like renting a car — you drive it wherever you need without worrying about engine maintenance or long-term ownership.
words.techContext: We launched a fleet of t3.medium EC2 instances to handle the processing of our batch jobs.
words.exampleSentence: We deployed our web application onto a fleet of t3.medium EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer.
words.etymology: Acronym for Elastic Compute Cloud (two Cs).

words.shortDefinition: Highly scalable object storage service for flat files and unstructured data.
words.simpleDefinition: Foundational cloud storage for files — acting as an infinitely scalable online hard drive. It stores data as objects within buckets, providing eleven nines of durability and high availability for web-scale applications.
words.longDefinition: Simple Storage Service (S3) is built to store and retrieve any amount of data from anywhere on the web. It is commonly used for hosting static website content, storing database backups, and serving as a data lake for big data analytics. Features like versioning, lifecycle policies, and cross-region replication allow for robust data management and disaster recovery strategies in complex architectures.
words.connector: Think of S3 like a massive self-storage unit — you pay only for the space used and can retrieve items instantly at any time.
words.techContext: All user-uploaded profile pictures are stored in an S3 bucket with restricted public access.
words.exampleSentence: We configured an S3 bucket to host our static React application and enabled CloudFront for global content delivery.
words.etymology: Acronym for Simple Storage Service (three Ss).

words.shortDefinition: Logically isolated virtual network for defining private sections of the AWS cloud.
words.simpleDefinition: Private section of the AWS cloud — define custom virtual networks with complete control over IP ranges, subnets, and route tables. It enables secure communication between resources while shielding them from the public internet.
words.longDefinition: A Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) provides complete control over your virtual networking environment, including resource placement, connectivity, and security. You can use subnets to partition your network and leverage Network Access Control Lists (NACLs) and Security Groups to control inbound and outbound traffic. VPC Peering and Transit Gateways allow for complex networking topologies across multiple accounts and regions within the global AWS infrastructure.
words.connector: Think of a VPC like a private gated community — you control exactly who enters through the gates and how residents communicate.
words.techContext: For security, we placed our database in a private subnet within our VPC so it's not accessible from the public internet.
words.exampleSentence: Our architecture places database instances in a private subnet within the VPC to prevent direct access from the internet.

words.shortDefinition: Secure management of access to AWS services and resources through permissions.
words.simpleDefinition: Identity and Access Management — the core service for controlling access through users, groups, and roles. It follows the principle of least privilege, providing granular control over every API action within your account.
words.longDefinition: IAM allows you to manage access by creating policies that define what actions are allowed or denied on specific resources. These policies can be attached to IAM users, groups, or roles. Roles are particularly powerful for granting temporary permissions to AWS services or federated users, eliminating the need to manage long-term credentials like access keys and reducing the risk of security breaches.
words.connector: Think of IAM like a building's security badge — it defines exactly which floors and rooms you are allowed to enter.
words.techContext: We created an IAM role for the Lambda function that only gives it permission to write to a specific DynamoDB table.
words.exampleSentence: We created an IAM role with read-only permissions for our S3 bucket and assigned it to the application's EC2 instance.
words.etymology: Acronym for Identity and Access Management.

words.shortDefinition: Serverless compute service that runs code in response to system events.
words.simpleDefinition: Serverless compute service — runs code in response to triggers like file uploads or API calls without managing servers. Pay only for the milliseconds of compute time consumed, scaling automatically with incoming request volume.
words.longDefinition: AWS Lambda executes your code only when needed and scales automatically, from a few requests per day to thousands per second. It supports multiple programming languages and integrates seamlessly with over 200 AWS services. By removing the need to provision or manage servers, Lambda allows developers to focus entirely on core business logic and enables highly responsive, event-driven architectures.
words.connector: Think of Lambda like a motion-sensing light — it only turns on when someone walks by, saving energy when the room is empty.
words.techContext: We used an AWS Lambda function to automatically resize images whenever they are uploaded to our S3 bucket.
words.exampleSentence: We used a Lambda function to automatically generate thumbnails whenever a new high-resolution image was uploaded to our S3 bucket.
words.etymology: Named after the 'lambda' symbol in calculus and functional programming.

words.shortDefinition: Managed service for setting up, operating, and scaling relational databases.
words.simpleDefinition: Managed relational database service — automates complex tasks like hardware provisioning, setup, patching, and backups for engines like PostgreSQL or MySQL. It provides high availability through Multi-AZ deployments and scales read traffic using read replicas.
words.longDefinition: Amazon Relational Database Service (RDS) makes it easy to operate and scale relational databases in the cloud. It provides cost-efficient and resizable capacity while managing time-consuming administration tasks, freeing you to focus on your applications. RDS supports six popular database engines, providing the performance and security features required for production workloads, including automated failover and encrypted storage at rest.
words.connector: Think of RDS like a professional laundry service — you drop off the clothes, and they handle the washing, drying, and folding.
words.techContext: By moving our MySQL database to RDS, we no longer have to manually manage OS updates or database backups.
words.exampleSentence: Our team migrated the on-premise MySQL database to Amazon RDS to benefit from automated backups and Multi-AZ high availability.

words.shortDefinition: Fully managed NoSQL database providing single-digit millisecond performance at scale.
words.simpleDefinition: Managed NoSQL database — delivers consistent, low-latency performance at any scale without manual sharding. It supports both key-value and document data models, automatically spreading data across multiple servers to handle massive throughput and high-traffic applications.
words.longDefinition: Amazon DynamoDB is a serverless, NoSQL database designed for applications that require consistent, single-digit millisecond latency at any scale. It features built-in security, continuous backups, and automated multi-region replication. With its flexible schema and support for ACID transactions, DynamoDB is widely used for mobile backends, gaming, ad tech, and any application requiring high-concurrency read and write operations.
words.connector: Think of DynamoDB like a high-speed library robot — it can find any single book in a millisecond, regardless of how many millions exist.
words.techContext: We chose DynamoDB for our user session store because it can handle millions of requests per second without any manual sharding.
words.exampleSentence: We used DynamoDB to store user session data because of its ability to handle millions of requests per second during peak hours.

words.shortDefinition: Scalable cloud Domain Name System for routing users to internet applications.
words.simpleDefinition: Highly available cloud DNS — translates human-readable domain names into numerical IP addresses. It also handles domain registration and provides sophisticated health checks and routing policies to ensure traffic reaches the healthiest and closest application endpoints globally.
words.longDefinition: Amazon Route 53 is a highly available and scalable Domain Name System (DNS) web service. It provides developers and businesses with a reliable way to route end users to internet applications. Route 53 effectively connects user requests to infrastructure running in AWS—such as EC2 instances, Elastic Load Balancers, or S3 buckets—and can also be used to route users to infrastructure outside of AWS.
words.connector: Think of Route 53 like a global GPS — you enter a destination name, and it gives you the fastest coordinates to get there.
words.techContext: We used Route 53 to set up a latency-based routing policy to send users to the AWS region closest to them.
words.exampleSentence: We configured Route 53 latency-based routing to automatically direct users to the AWS region that provides the lowest network delay.
words.etymology: Reference to Port 53 (the standard DNS port) + 'Route' (navigation).

words.shortDefinition: Global Content Delivery Network for securely delivering data with low latency.
words.simpleDefinition: Global Content Delivery Network (CDN) — securely delivers data, videos, and APIs to users with low latency. It caches content at edge locations worldwide, reducing the physical distance between users and servers while offloading traffic from origin resources.
words.longDefinition: Amazon CloudFront is a fast content delivery network service that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to customers globally. It integrates with other AWS services to give developers an easy way to accelerate content delivery. CloudFront uses a global network of edge locations and regional edge caches to provide high-performance delivery, ensuring that users receive content from the nearest possible location.
words.connector: Think of CloudFront like a chain of local bookstores — they keep popular titles in stock so you don't wait for shipping.
words.techContext: By putting CloudFront in front of our S3 bucket, we reduced image load times for our users in Europe by over 60%.
words.exampleSentence: We used CloudFront to distribute our video assets globally, significantly reducing buffering times for our international users in Asia and Europe.

words.shortDefinition: Managed service for creating, publishing, and securing APIs at any scale.
words.simpleDefinition: Front door for applications — manage, secure, and scale APIs that connect to backend services like Lambda or EC2. It handles thousands of concurrent calls, providing traffic management, authorization, and throttling to protect your internal infrastructure.
words.longDefinition: Amazon API Gateway is a fully managed service that makes it easy for developers to create, publish, maintain, monitor, and secure APIs at any scale. It acts as a "front door" for applications to access data, business logic, or functionality from your backend services. API Gateway handles all the tasks involved in accepting and processing up to hundreds of thousands of concurrent API calls.
words.connector: Think of API Gateway like a building's receptionist — they check your ID, take your message, and direct you to the right office.
words.techContext: We used API Gateway to expose our Lambda functions as a RESTful API for our mobile app.
words.exampleSentence: We exposed our backend microservices through API Gateway to implement rate limiting and request validation for our public-facing mobile application.

words.shortDefinition: Managed message queuing service for decoupling and scaling distributed software systems.
words.simpleDefinition: Fully managed message queuing — decouple and scale microservices or serverless applications. It provides a reliable buffer to store messages until they can be processed asynchronously, preventing data loss when backend services are overwhelmed or experience temporary downtime.
words.longDefinition: Amazon Simple Queue Service (SQS) is a fully managed message queuing service that enables you to decouple and scale microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications. SQS eliminates the complexity and overhead associated with managing and operating message-oriented middleware, and empowers developers to focus on differentiating work. You can send, store, and receive messages between software components at any volume.
words.connector: Think of SQS like a restaurant's order ticket rail — the chef pulls tickets and cooks them one by one as they have capacity.
words.techContext: When a user places an order, we push a message to an SQS queue so the processing service can handle it asynchronously.
words.exampleSentence: We used an SQS queue to buffer incoming image processing requests, allowing our worker instances to process them at a steady rate.

words.shortDefinition: Managed pub/sub messaging service for high-throughput, push-based delivery to subscribers.
words.simpleDefinition: Managed pub/sub messaging — provides high-throughput, push-based delivery between publishers and many subscribers. It allows applications to send messages to multiple endpoints simultaneously, facilitating real-time notifications via Lambda, email, SMS, or mobile push for distributed architectures.
words.longDefinition: Amazon Simple Notification Service (SNS) is a highly available, durable, secure, fully managed pub/sub messaging service that enables you to decouple microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications. SNS provides topics for high-throughput, push-based, many-to-many messaging. Using SNS topics, your publisher systems can fan out messages to a large number of subscriber endpoints including SQS queues, Lambda functions, and HTTP/S webhooks.
words.connector: Think of SNS like a community loudspeaker — one person makes an announcement, and everyone who is listening hears it at the same time.
words.techContext: We use SNS to broadcast events like 'new_user_registered' to multiple microservices simultaneously.
words.exampleSentence: When a new user signs up, we publish a message to an SNS topic to trigger both a welcome email and a data analytics update.

words.shortDefinition: High-performance block storage service for use with Amazon EC2 instances.
words.simpleDefinition: Persistent block storage for EC2 — acting like a raw, unformatted hard drive that can be attached to servers. EBS volumes offer high durability and availability, surviving instance termination and providing the performance needed for intensive database workloads.
words.longDefinition: Amazon Elastic Block Store (EBS) provides high-performance block storage volumes for use with Amazon EC2 instances. EBS volumes are highly available and reliable storage volumes that can be attached to any running instance in the same Availability Zone. They are particularly suited for applications that require a database, a file system, or access to raw block-level storage, offering consistent and low-latency performance.
words.connector: Think of EBS like a portable USB hard drive — you can plug it into any computer, save your work, and take it with you.
words.techContext: We upgraded our EBS volume to an 'io2' type to provide higher IOPS for our database server.
words.exampleSentence: We attached an EBS 'io2' volume to our production database instance to ensure we had enough IOPS for high-speed transaction processing.

words.shortDefinition: Managed service for running and scaling Kubernetes clusters in the AWS cloud.
words.simpleDefinition: Managed Kubernetes service — run containerized applications without installing or operating your own control plane. It automates critical tasks like patching, node updates, and scaling, ensuring your Kubernetes environment is secure and highly available for production traffic.
words.longDefinition: Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) is a managed service that makes it easy for you to run Kubernetes on AWS without needing to install, operate, and maintain your own Kubernetes control plane or nodes. Kubernetes is an open-source system for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. EKS is certified Kubernetes conformant, so you can use existing tools and plugins from the Kubernetes community.
words.connector: Think of EKS like a car lease — you get to drive the car anywhere you want, but the dealership handles the major engine repairs.
words.techContext: Moving from self-managed Kubernetes to EKS reduced our operational overhead by handling Control Plane upgrades automatically.
words.exampleSentence: Our DevOps team migrated our microservices to EKS to leverage the Kubernetes ecosystem while offloading the control plane management to AWS.

words.shortDefinition: Fully managed container orchestration service for running Docker containers at scale.
words.simpleDefinition: Highly scalable container orchestration — facilitates running and managing Docker containers on AWS. It offers a simpler alternative to Kubernetes, deeply integrating with the AWS ecosystem for seamless management of security, networking, and load balancing for containerized workloads.
words.longDefinition: Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS) is a highly scalable, high-performance container orchestration service that supports Docker containers and allows you to easily run and scale containerized applications on AWS. ECS eliminates the need for you to install and operate your own container orchestration software, manage and scale a cluster of virtual machines, or schedule containers on those virtual machines.
words.connector: Think of ECS like a warehouse manager — they decide exactly where each shipping container should go to make the best use of space.
words.techContext: For our smaller microservices, we chose ECS over Kubernetes because of its tighter integration with other AWS services like IAM and CloudWatch.
words.exampleSentence: We chose ECS for our web service because its tight integration with IAM and Route 53 simplified our infrastructure deployment and management.

words.shortDefinition: Serverless compute engine for containers that removes the need to manage servers.
words.simpleDefinition: Serverless compute for containers — run Dockerized applications without managing underlying EC2 infrastructure. Define CPU and memory requirements, and AWS handles all provisioning and scaling, allowing you to pay only for the resources used by your specific containers.
words.longDefinition: AWS Fargate is a serverless, pay-as-you-go compute engine that lets you focus on building applications without managing servers. Fargate works with both Amazon ECS and Amazon EKS. When you run your tasks and services with the Fargate launch type, you package your application in containers, specify the CPU and memory requirements, define networking and IAM policies, and launch the application.
words.connector: Think of Fargate like a taxi service — you just tell them the destination and you don't have to worry about maintaining the car.
words.techContext: By using Fargate, we eliminated the need to patch or scale the servers in our cluster—we just focus on the container code.
words.exampleSentence: By moving our containerized tasks to Fargate, we eliminated the operational burden of patching and scaling the underlying EC2 instances in our cluster.
words.etymology: Invented name, possibly implying 'far' reaching 'gate' way to compute.

words.shortDefinition: Monitoring and observability service for AWS resources and applications in real-time.
words.simpleDefinition: Monitoring and observability service — collects logs, metrics, and events to provide a unified view of resource health. It enables developers to set alarms and visualize performance data, facilitating proactive issue detection and automated responses to system changes.
words.longDefinition: Amazon CloudWatch monitors your Amazon Web Services (AWS) resources and the applications you run on AWS in real time. You can use CloudWatch to collect and track metrics, which are variables you can measure for your resources and applications. CloudWatch provides you with data and actionable insights to monitor your applications, respond to system-wide performance changes, and optimize resource utilization.
words.connector: Think of CloudWatch like a hospital heart monitor — it tracks vital signs and sounds an alarm if anything goes out of a healthy range.
words.techContext: I set up a CloudWatch Alarm to notify the team via Slack if the CPU usage on the production instances exceeds 80% for 5 minutes.
words.exampleSentence: I configured a CloudWatch alarm to notify our on-call engineer whenever the application's error rate exceeds 5% for more than two minutes.

codeExample.code:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "foobar" {
  alarm_name          = "cb-cpu-alarm"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = "2"
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  namespace           = "AWS/EC2"
  period              = "120"
  statistic           = "Average"
  threshold           = "80"
}

words.shortDefinition: Infrastructure as Code service for modeling and provisioning AWS resource collections.
words.simpleDefinition: Native Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — model and provision entire AWS architectures using simple template files. It ensures infrastructure is deployed in an orderly and predictable fashion, allowing teams to version control their environments and automate resource management.
words.longDefinition: AWS CloudFormation gives you an easy way to model a collection of related AWS and third-party resources, provision them quickly and consistently, and manage them throughout their lifecycles, by treating infrastructure as code. A CloudFormation template describes your desired resources and their dependencies so you can launch and configure them together as a single stack.
words.connector: Think of CloudFormation like an automated assembly line — you provide the blueprints, and it builds the entire factory exactly to your specifications.
words.techContext: We use a CloudFormation stack to define our entire networking infrastructure, ensuring we can recreate it in any region.
words.exampleSentence: We use CloudFormation stacks to ensure our staging and production environments are identical, reducing the risk of configuration drift between deployments.

codeExample.code:
Resources:
  MyS3Bucket:
    Type: 'AWS::S3::Bucket'
    Properties:
      BucketName: my-cloudformation-bucket

words.shortDefinition: Software development framework for defining cloud infrastructure using familiar programming languages.
words.simpleDefinition: Cloud Development Kit — define infrastructure using high-level programming languages like TypeScript or Python. It translates code into CloudFormation templates, enabling developers to use loops, logic, and object-oriented practices to build complex cloud architectures more efficiently.
words.longDefinition: The AWS Cloud Development Kit (CDK) is an open-source software development framework to define your cloud infrastructure in code and provision it through AWS CloudFormation. It provides high-level components called constructs that preconfigure cloud resources with proven defaults, helping you build on AWS faster. Developers can use their existing skills and familiar tools to build cloud applications.
words.connector: Think of the CDK like an architect using advanced CAD software — it allows for much more complex and automated designs than hand-drawn sketches.
words.techContext: Using the AWS CDK allowed us to use loops and logic to generate our infrastructure, which is much more powerful than raw YAML.
words.exampleSentence: Using the AWS CDK, I wrote a reusable construct that automatically sets up a secure VPC with public and private subnets across multiple zones.

codeExample.code:
import * as s3 from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-s3';
new s3.Bucket(this, 'MyFirstBucket', { versioned: true });

words.shortDefinition: Managed in-memory data store service for improving application performance through caching.
words.simpleDefinition: Managed in-memory data store — supports Redis and Memcached to improve web application performance through sub-millisecond access. It offloads pressure from primary databases by storing frequently accessed data in RAM, significantly reducing latency for high-traffic workloads.
words.longDefinition: Amazon ElastiCache is a fully managed in-memory data store and cache service by Amazon Web Services. The service improves the performance of web applications by retrieving information from managed in-memory caches, instead of relying entirely on slower disk-based databases. It allows you to seamlessly set up, run, and scale popular open-source compatible in-memory data stores in the cloud.
words.connector: Think of ElastiCache like a pocket dictionary — it keeps the most common words right at hand so you don't have to visit the library.
words.techContext: We used ElastiCache with Redis to cache our most expensive database queries, reducing our average page load time by 300ms.
words.exampleSentence: We implemented ElastiCache with Redis to store user session tokens, which reduced our database load and improved the overall responsiveness of our API.

words.shortDefinition: Fast, managed data warehouse for large-scale data analysis using standard SQL.
words.simpleDefinition: Fast, managed data warehouse — designed for complex analytical queries on petabyte-scale datasets. It leverages columnar storage and parallel processing to deliver high performance, allowing businesses to analyze massive amounts of structured data using familiar SQL-based tools.
words.longDefinition: Amazon Redshift is a fast, fully managed data warehouse that makes it simple and cost-effective to analyze all your data using standard SQL and your existing Business Intelligence (BI) tools. It allows you to run complex analytic queries against petabytes of structured data, using sophisticated query optimization, columnar storage on high-performance storage, and massively parallel query execution.
words.connector: Think of Redshift like a specialized research archive — it's built for looking at massive trends across history rather than looking up one single fact.
words.techContext: We migrated our historical sales data into Redshift so our data scientists can run complex analytical queries without affecting the production database.
words.exampleSentence: Our data engineering team uses Redshift to aggregate millions of daily transaction records into weekly reports for the business intelligence dashboard.

codeExample.code:
resource "aws_redshift_cluster" "example" {
  cluster_identifier = "tf-redshift-cluster"
  database_name      = "mydb"
  master_username    = "foo"
  master_password    = "Mustbe8characters"
  node_type          = "dc2.large"
  cluster_type       = "single-node"
}

words.etymology: Named after the 'redshift' phenomenon in astronomy (moving away), possibly implying moving away from Oracle (Big Red).

words.shortDefinition: Scalable platform for real-time data streaming and analysis on AWS.
words.simpleDefinition: Real-time data streaming platform — ingest and analyze massive volumes of data as it arrives. It handles continuous flows from website clickstreams, financial transactions, and IoT sensors, enabling developers to build responsive applications that react instantly to live information.
words.longDefinition: Amazon Kinesis makes it easy to collect, process, and analyze real-time, streaming data so you can get timely insights and react quickly to new information. Amazon Kinesis offers key capabilities to cost-effectively process streaming data at any scale, along with the flexibility to choose the tools that best suit the requirements of your application.
words.connector: Think of Kinesis like a high-volume industrial conveyor belt — it moves a constant stream of items through the factory for immediate processing.
words.techContext: We use Kinesis Data Firehose to stream all of our application logs directly into an S3 bucket for long-term storage.
words.exampleSentence: We used Kinesis Data Streams to capture and process real-time clickstream data from our website to provide personalized product recommendations to users.

words.shortDefinition: Service for securely storing, rotating, and managing sensitive application credentials.
words.simpleDefinition: Secure credential management — store and rotate sensitive data like database passwords and API keys. It allows applications to retrieve secrets at runtime via an API, eliminating the risk of hardcoding plain-text passwords in source code or configuration files.
words.longDefinition: AWS Secrets Manager helps you protect secrets needed to access your applications, services, and IT resources. The service enables you to easily rotate, manage, and retrieve database credentials, API keys, and other secrets throughout their lifecycle. Users and applications retrieve secrets with a call to Secrets Manager APIs, eliminating the need to hardcode sensitive information in plain text.
words.connector: Think of Secrets Manager like a high-tech digital safe — it stores your most valuable keys and automatically changes the locks every thirty days.
words.techContext: Instead of storing the database password in a config file, our application retrieves it at runtime from AWS Secrets Manager.
words.exampleSentence: Instead of hardcoding the database credentials, our Lambda function retrieves the encrypted password from Secrets Manager at the start of every execution.

words.shortDefinition: Centralized service for creating and managing cryptographic keys for data encryption.
words.simpleDefinition: Key Management Service — used to create and control cryptographic keys for encrypting data across AWS. It provides a centralized hub for managing key lifecycles and integrates with CloudTrail to provide detailed logs of all key usage for compliance.
words.longDefinition: AWS Key Management Service (KMS) makes it easy for you to create and manage cryptographic keys and control their use across a wide range of AWS services and in your applications. AWS KMS is a secure and resilient service that uses hardware security modules that have been validated under FIPS 140-2 to protect your keys.
words.connector: Think of KMS like the master key system for a hotel — it manages every single room key from one central, highly secure location.
words.techContext: We use a KMS customer master key (CMK) to encrypt all our sensitive data at rest in S3 and RDS.
words.exampleSentence: We used a KMS customer managed key to ensure that all data stored in our S3 buckets is encrypted at rest using our own keys.

words.shortDefinition: Collection of EC2 instances that automatically adjusts size based on demand.
words.simpleDefinition: Dynamic instance collection — automatically adjusts its size based on real-time traffic demand or scheduled conditions. It ensures the application has the correct number of servers running, maximizing availability while minimizing costs by scaling down during low usage periods.
words.longDefinition: An Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group contains a collection of Amazon EC2 instances that are treated as a logical grouping for the purposes of automatic scaling and management. An Auto Scaling group also enables you to use Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling features such as health check replacements and scaling policies. Maintaining the number of instances in an Auto Scaling group ensures that your application is always running.
words.connector: Think of an Autoscaling Group like an accordion — it expands to handle more volume and contracts when the music slows down to save space.
words.techContext: Our Autoscaling Group added three new instances in response to the increased latency detected by the load balancer.
words.exampleSentence: We configured our Autoscaling Group to maintain a minimum of two instances across different availability zones to ensure high availability for our production website.
